description: Chrysojasminum fruticans, the wild jasmine or common yellow jasmine, is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae, native to the Mediterranean region and the Middle East as far as northern Iran. A semi-evergreen shrub reaching 1.5 m (5 ft) with yellow flowers and black berries, it is available from commercial suppliers.
origin: Mediterranean to Northern Iran

description: Chrysojasminum humile, the Italian jasmine or yellow jasmine, is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae, native to Afghanistan, Tajikistan, Pakistan, Nepal, Burma (Myanmar), the Himalayas and south west China. The species is widely cultivated and reportedly naturalized in Greece, Sicily and the former Yugoslavia.
origin: Southern Iran to Central China
synonyms: ["Jasminum humile"]

description: Forestiera acuminata, commonly known as eastern swamp privet, is a deciduous shrub or small tree that is native to the southeastern and central United States, growing primarily in or near wetlands. It is especially common along the Mississippi Valley as far north as Illinois and Indiana, but found also across the South from eastern Texas to South Carolina. It grows in swamp forests as well as rocky edges of streams and ponds.
origin: LOU OO, SCA OO, FLA OO, MSI OO, FLA
synonyms: ["Adelia ovata", "Carpoxis spinosa", "Borya acuminata", "Forestiera cuneifolia", "Borya retusa", "Adelia acuminata", "Borya prinoides", "Forestiera retusa", "Bigelovia acuminata", "Carpoxis inermis"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Forestiera pubescens, commonly known as stretchberry, desert olive, tanglewood, devil's elbow, elbow bush, spring goldenglow, spring herald, New Mexico privet, or Texas forsythia is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico.
origin: MEXICO, NEV, MXN BC, MXE ZA, NORTHWESTERN USA
synonyms: ["Forestiera ligustrina var. pubescens", "Adelia pubescens"]

care.light_guide: Climate: subtropical

description: Forestiera segregata is a species of flowering plant in the olive family known by the common names Florida privet, Florida swampprivet, and southern privet. It is native to the Bahamas, the Greater Antilles, including Puerto Rico and the Cayman Islands, the Lesser Antilles, including Anguilla, and Florida, Georgia, and South Carolina in the United States.
Forsythia suspensa, commonly known as weeping forsythia or golden-bell, is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae. It is native to China.
origin: China, East Asia
synonyms: ["Ligustrum suspensum", "Forsythia suspensa f. aureovariegata", "Forsythia suspensa f. atrocaulis", "Forsythia suspensa f. decipiens", "Forsythia suspensa f. pubescens", "Forsythia suspensa var. latifolia", "Forsythia suspensa f. pallida", "Forsythia fortunei", "Rangium suspensum", "Lilac perpensa"]

description: Fraxinus albicans, commonly called the Texas ash, is a species of tree in the olive family (Oleaceae). It is native to North America, where it is found from eastern Texas and southern Oklahoma in the United States, to the state of Durango in Mexico. Its natural habitat is in dry, rocky slopes, often over limestone.
origin: MXE CU, MXE HI, MEXICO, MXE ZA, MXE GU
synonyms: ["Fraxinus texensis", "Fraxinus americana var. albicans", "Fraxinus americana var. texensis", "Fraxinus americana subsp. texensis"]

care.light_guide: Climate: subtropical

description: Male flowers whither away while fertilized female flowers give way to drooping, 6 to 8 inch long clusters of one winged dry flattened samara (to 2 inches long) that ripen in fall and may persist on the tree throughout the winter. Fruit displays from August to October.
origin: Eastern North America and Mexico
synonyms: ["Fraxinus americana f. iodocarpa", "Fraxinus americana var. epiptera", "Fraxinus juglandifolia var. subserrata", "Fraxinus americana var. acuminata", "Fraxinus pubescens var. latifolia", "Calycomelia alba", "Fraxinus americana f. barrii", "Calycomelia pistaciifolia", "Aplilia macrophyla", "Fraxinus americana subsp. biltmoreana"]

common_pests: Planting new ash trees is no longer recommended due to the trees' susceptibility to the emerald ash borer, a pest that feeds under the bark and bores into the wood.
Emerald ash borer will typically kill an ash tree within 3 to 5 years after infestation and, once infestation occurs, it is difficult to eradicate.
Other potential problems are redheaded ash borer, lilac borer, carpenter worm, oyster shell scale, leaf miners, fall webworms, ash sawflies, Ash Flower Gall Mite and ash leaf cur
care.used_for: ["The juice from leaves used on mosquito bites for relief of swelling and itching and are a prophylactic measure for snake bites. care.used_for_details: The juice from leaves used on mosquito bites for relief of swelling and itching and are a prophylactic measure for snake bites. description: Fraxinus angustifolia, the narrow-leaved ash, is a species of Fraxinus native to Central Europe and Southern Europe, Northwest Africa, and Southwest Asia.
origin: ITA VC, IRQ OO, AFG OO, NCS ST, KRY OO
synonyms: ["Fraxinus excelsior subsp. angustifolia", "Fraxinus excelsior var. angustifolia", "Fraxinus oxyphylla subsp. angustifolia", "Fraxinus excelsior proles angustifolia", "Fraxinus oxycarpa var. angustifolia"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Fraxinus anomala is a species of ash tree known by the common name single-leaf ash. It is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, where it grows in a number of habitats including desert scrub and chaparral. It is unusual in the genus in that some specimens have simple leaves instead of the pinnate leaves more characteristic of the group.
origin: UTA, NORTHWESTERN USA, NWM OO, NEV OO, NWM

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Fraxinus caroliniana, the pop ash, Florida ash, swamp ash, Carolina ash, or water ash, is a species of ash tree native from Cuba through the subtropical Southeastern United States from southern Virginia to Texas. It was originally described by the botanist Philip Miller. It is a small tree about 40 ft. Leaves are compound, opposite, 7–12 in long, leaflets 5–7 in, ovate to oblong, coarsely serrate or entire, 3–6 in long, 2–3 in wide.
origin: Southeastern United States to Cuba
synonyms: ["Fraxinus platycarpa var. floridana", "Leptalix caroliniana", "Fraxinus platycarpa var. triptera", "Fraxinus pleurodes", "Leptalix pallida", "Samarpses triptera", "Fraxinus pennsylvanica var. cubensis", "Fraxinus palustris", "Fraxinus pennsylvanica subsp. cubensis", "Fraxinus platycarpa"]

description: Fraxinus chinensis, the Chinese ash, is a species of ash tree. Its leaves are used in traditional Chinese medicine for dysentery disorders, and it can be used as cultivator of the white scale insects for candle making.
origin: PUE, CHN SA, CHS HN, JAP HN, CHS AH

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Fraxinus dipetala, the California ash or two-petal ash, is a species of ash native to southwestern North America in the United States in northwestern Arizona, California, southern Nevada, and Utah, and in Mexico in northern Baja California. It grows at altitudes of 100–1,300 m.
origin: MXN, MXN SO, UTA, NEV OO, ARI OO
synonyms: ["Fraxinus trifoliolata", "Petlomelia dipetala", "Fraxinus dipetala var. trifoliolata", "Chionanthus fraxinifolia", "Fraxinus parryi", "Fraxinus schiedei", "Fraxinus jonesii", "Fraxinus ciliata", "Ornus dipetala", "Ornus quadrialata"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Fraxinus excelsior, known as the ash, or European ash or common ash to distinguish it from other types of ash, is a flowering plant species in the olive family Oleaceae. It is native throughout mainland Europe east to the Caucasus and Alborz mountains, and west to Great Britain and Ireland, the latter determining its western boundary. The northernmost location is in the Trondheimsfjord region of Norway.
origin: ITA VC, TCS AR, KRY OO, NCS ST, EASTERN CANADA
synonyms: ["Fraxinus excelsior var. communis"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate
care.toxicity_note: Human: toxic (unknown strength). Animal: toxic. Parts: Fruit, leaf (or whole fruit). (Source: TPPT/Agroscope)

description: Fraxinus lanuginosa is a species of ash native to Japan and to the Primorye region of eastern Russia.
Female trees produce winged samara with a single seed and single wing in hanging clusters. They are 1- 2 inches long
origin: Southwestern Canada to California

description: Fraxinus longicuspis, the taper-tip ash or Japanese ash, is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae, native to the mountains of central and southern Japan. A deciduous tree, it is hardy to USDA zone 5. It is used as a street tree in a few cities around the world.
origin: JAP SH, JAP HK, JAP HN, EASTERN ASIA, JAP
synonyms: ["Fraxinus caudata", "Fraxinus pubinervis", "Fraxinus kantoensis", "Fraxinus bungeana var. obovata", "Fraxinus obovata", "Fraxinus koehneana", "Fraxinus pubinervis", "Fraxinus chinensis var. acuminata", "Fraxinus satsumana", "Fraxinus bungeana var. pubinervis"]

description: Fraxinus mandshurica, or the Manchurian ash, is a species of Fraxinus native to northeastern Asia in northern China, Korea, Japan and southeastern Russia.
origin: PRM OO, CHN TJ, CHC CQ, CHI NM, CHN GS
synonyms: ["Fraxinus elatior", "Fraxinus nigra subsp. mandshurica", "Fraxinus mandshurica var. japonica", "Fraxinus mammifera", "Fraxinus nigra var. mandshurica", "Fraxinus excelsissima", "Fraxinus mandshurica subsp. brevipedicellata", "Fraxinus excelsa"]

description: Fraxinus nigra, or the black ash, is a species of ash native to much of eastern Canada and the northeastern United States, from western Newfoundland west to southeastern Manitoba, and south to Illinois and northern Virginia. Formerly abundant, as of 2017 the species is threatened with near total extirpation throughout its range within the next century as a result of infestation by an invasive parasitic insect known as the emerald ash borer.
origin: South Eastern Canada, North Central and North Eastern U.S.A.
synonyms: ["Fraxinus sambucifolia var. cucullata", "Leptalix nigra", "Fraxinus sambucifolia", "Fraxinus americana var. sambucifolia", "Calycomelia nigra", "Fraxinus americana var. nigra", "Fraxinus nigra var. cucullata", "Fraxinus nigra var. sambucifolia", "Fraxinoides nigra", "Fraxinus nigra f. cucullata"]

description: Fraxinus ornus, the manna ash or South European flowering ash, is a species of Fraxinus native to Southern Europe and Southwestern Asia, from Spain and Italy north to Austria and the Czech Republic, and east through the Balkans, Turkey, and western Syria to Lebanon and Armenia.
origin: YUG, IRE NI, POR OO, SPA AN, YUG MA
synonyms: ["Ornus ornus", "Fraxinus montana", "Ornus europaea"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Fraxinus pennsylvanica, the green ash or red ash, is a species of ash native to eastern and central North America, from Nova Scotia west to southeastern Alberta and eastern Colorado, south to northern Florida, and southwest to Oklahoma and eastern Texas. It has spread and become naturalized in much of the western United States and also in Argentina and Europe, from Spain to Russia.
origin: Central and Eastern Canada to West Central and Eastern US
synonyms: ["Leptalix ovata", "Fraxinus oblongocarpa", "Fraxinus americana var. subpubescens", "Fraxinus darlingtonii", "Fraxinus pennsylvanica f. erythrocarpa", "Fraxinus platyphylla", "Fraxinus americana var. normale", "Leptalix richardii", "Fraxinus pennsylvanica var. austinii", "Fraxinus lanceolata var. viridis"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
common_problems: Potential disease problems include fungal leaf spots, powdery mildew, rust, anthracnose, cankers, and ash yellows.
General ash decline is also a concern.
Brittle branches are susceptible to damage from high winds and snow/ice.
See potential insect and disease problem factsheets
common_pests: Green ash trees are generally susceptible to several insect problems including the Emerald ash borer, lilac borer, carpenter worm, oyster shell scale, leaf miners, fall webworms, ash sawflies, ash leaf curl aphids.
See potential insect and disease problem factsheets
care.used_for: ["Wood is heavy", "hard", "strong", "and coarse-grained. The wood is used to make tool handles", "oars", "sports equipment", "wood floors", "and furniture."]
care.used_for_details: Wood is heavy, hard, strong, and coarse-grained. The wood is used to make tool handles, oars, sports equipment, wood floors, and furniture.
care.edible_parts: Bark can be used in cooking as a thickener for soups or mixed with grain in making bread.

description: On female trees, drooping clusters of one-winged, flattened samara, 2 to 3 inches long, 1/2 inch wide, wing extends nearly to base, wider than green or white ash.
origin: Northern Central and Eastern North America.
synonyms: ["Fraxinus tomentosa", "Fraxinus pennsylvanica subsp. profunda", "Fraxinus tomentosa", "Fraxinus americana var. profunda", "Fraxinus michauxii", "Calycomelia tomentosa", "Calycomelia profunda", "Fraxinus profunda var. ashei", "Fraxinus pennsylvanica var. profunda"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate

description: Fraxinus quadrangulata, the blue ash, is a species of ash native primarily to the Midwestern United States from Oklahoma to Michigan, as well as the Bluegrass region of Kentucky and the Nashville Basin region of Tennessee. Isolated populations exist in Alabama, Southern Ontario, and small sections of the Appalachian Mountains. It is typically found over calcareous substrates such as limestone, growing on limestone slopes and in moist valley soils, at elevations of 120–600 m (390–1,970 ft).
origin: North and East central U.S.A.
synonyms: ["Fraxinus quadrangulata var. subpubescens", "Calycomelia quadrangulata", "Leptalix quadrangulata", "Fraxinus tetragona", "Fraxinus quadrangularis", "Fraxinus americana var. quadrangulata"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
common_problems: It can be more difficult to propagate and slower to establish than other ash trees.

common_pests: Emerald ash borer.
See potential insect and disease problem factsheets to the left.

description: Fraxinus sieboldiana, the Chinese flowering ash or Japanese flowering ash, is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae. It is native to southeastern China, the Korean peninsula, and Japan. It grows on wooded slopes and by streams. It is hardy to USDA zone 6. A variegated cultivar, 'Rising Sun', is available.
origin: CHS MA, CHS HK, JAP, CHINA, JAP KY

description: Fraxinus uhdei, commonly known as tropical ash or Shamel ash, is a species of tree native to Mexico and Central America. It is commonly planted as a street tree in Mexico and the southwestern United States. It has also been planted and spread from cultivation in Hawaii, where it is now considered an invasive species.

It is native to insular Southeast Asia from Java to the Philippines, as well as Australia, as well as some islands in the Pacific. Jasminum didymum occurs naturally in habitats from rainforests to arid and semi-arid shrublands.

description: Jasminum nudiflorum, the winter jasmine, is a slender, deciduous shrub native to China. The flower's blossoming peaks right after winter, which is why it is also named Yingchun (迎春) in Chinese, which means "the flower that welcomes Spring". It is widely cultivated as an ornamental and is reportedly naturalized in France and in scattered locations in the United States.
origin: South East Tibet to Central China

description: Jasminum officinale, known as the common jasmine or simply jasmine, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family Oleaceae. It is native to the Caucasus and parts of Asia, also widely naturalized.
origin: Turky through the Trans-caucasus to southern central China

description: Jasminum sambac is a species of jasmine native to Bhutan and India. It is cultivated in many places, especially West Asia, South Asia and Southeast Asia. It is naturalised in many scattered locales: Mauritius, Madagascar, the Maldives, Christmas Island, Chiapas, Central America, southern Florida, the Bahamas, Cuba, Hispaniola, Jamaica, Puerto Rico, and the Lesser Antilles.
origin: IND KE, EHM AP, NIC, VAN, IND CH
synonyms: ["Jasminum undulatum", "Jasminum blancoi", "Nyctanthes undulata", "Jasminum quinqueflorum var. pubescens", "Jasminum sambac var. trifoliatum", "Jasminum sambac var. verum", "Jasminum sambac var. normale", "Jasminum sambac var. nemocalyx", "Mogorium gimea", "Jasminum quadrifolium"]

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical

origin: LSI, MYA, VAN, NWG IJ, LSI LS
synonyms: ["Jasminum australe"]

care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical

description: Ligustrum japonicum, known as wax-leaf privet or Japanese privet is a species of Ligustrum (privet) native to central and southern Japan and Korea. It is widely cultivated in other regions, and is naturalized in California and in the southeastern United States from Texas to Virginia.
origin: South East China to Temp. Eastern Asia
synonyms: ["Ligustrum syringiflorum", "Ligustrum japonicum f. leucocarpum", "Ligustridium japonicum", "Ligustrum japonicum var. crassifolium", "Ligustrum japonicum f. tricolor", "Ligustrum glabrum", "Ligustrum syringifolium", "Ligustrum taquetii", "Ligustrum japonicum var. ovalifolium", "Ligustrum latifolium"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Gastrointestinal upset (most common), incoordination, increased heart rate, death (rare).
common_problems: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale and privet rust mites are common insect problems.
Anthracnose, twig blight, cankers, powdery mildew and root rot are common disease problems.
Susceptible to copper deficiency.
Invasive.
See native or less problematic suggestions to the left.
common_pests: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale and privet rust mites are common insect problems.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Gastrointestinal upset (most common), incoordination, increased heart rate, death (rare).
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.

description: Ligustrum lucidum, the broad-leaf privet, Chinese privet, glossy privet, tree privet or wax-leaf privet, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family Oleaceae, native to the southern half of China and naturalized in many places. The name "Chinese privet" is also used for Ligustrum sinense.
origin: Central   Southern China to Hainan and Southern Korea
synonyms: ["Ligustrum japonicum var. macrophyllum", "Ligustrum wallichii", "Ligustrum japonicum var. alivonii", "Olea clavata", "Ligustrum lucidum f. aureomarginatum", "Ligustrum lucidum f. tricolor", "Ligustrum lucidum var. tricolor", "Ligustrum japonicum f. aureomarginatum", "Ligustrum lucidum f. latifolium", "Ligustrum esquirolii"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
common_problems: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale, and privet rust mites are common insect problems.
Anthracnose, twig blight, cankers, powdery mildew, and root rot are common disease problems.
Susceptible to copper deficiency.
It can be weedy in disturbed areas and has escaped and naturalized in moist areas.
It is considered invasive by the NC Invasive Plant Council.
The berries produced by this plant are toxic.

common_pests: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale, and privet rust mites are common insect problems.

description: Ligustrum obtusifolium is a species of privet, native to Japan, Korea and northeastern China. The species is considered invasive in parts of the United States. It has become very common in southern New England, the mid-Atlantic States, and the Great Lakes regions, with scattered occurrences in the South, the Great Plains, and Washington state. With Ligustrum ovalifolium it is a parent of the widespread hybrid Ligustrum × ibolium.
origin: VER OO, NWY, CHN TJ, TEN OO, NORTH CENTRAL USA
synonyms: ["Ligustrum suave", "Ligustrum ibota subvar. spathulatum", "Ligustrum ibota f. obtusifolium", "Ligustrum ibota f. angustifolium", "Ligustrum ibota f. tschonoskii", "Ligustrum ibota var. tschonoskii", "Ligustrum ibota var. angustifolium", "Ligustrum ibota var. microphyllum", "Ligustrum obtusifolium var. rubescens", "Ligustrum obtusifolium f. velutinum"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Ligustrum ovalifolium, also known as Korean privet, California privet, garden privet, and oval-leaved privet, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family Oleaceae. The species is native to Japan and Korea.
origin: IRE NI, MEXICO, BAL, NWY, POR OO
synonyms: ["Ligustrum japonicum var. ovalifolium"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Ligustrum quihoui, or waxyleaf privet, is a shrub native to Korea and China. As with some other members of the genus, L. quihoui is cultivated as an ornamental in many places and has become naturalized and invasive in urban areas and scattered forested locales of the southeastern United States.
origin: CHN TJ, CHC CQ, CHI NM, NORTH CENTRAL USA, CHN GS
synonyms: ["Ligustrum argyi", "Ligustrum quihoui var. latifolium", "Ligustrum vulgare var. brachystachyum", "Ligustrum quihoui var. brachystachyum", "Ligustrum brachystachyum"]

care.light_guide: Climate: temperate

description: Ligustrum sinense is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae. This privet is native to China, Taiwan and Vietnam, and invasive in Réunion, the Andaman Islands, Norfolk Island, Costa Rica, Honduras, Panama and much of the eastern and southern United States. The name "Chinese privet" may also refer to Ligustrum lucidum.
origin: Eurasia

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day) | Climate: subtropical

description: Ligustrum vulgare is a species of Ligustrum native to central and southern Europe, north Africa and southwestern Asia, from Ireland and southwestern Sweden south to Morocco, and east to Poland and northwestern Iran.
origin: Eurasia
synonyms: ["Ligustrum vulgare f. pendulum", "Ligustrum vulgare var. lactiflorum", "Ligustrum vulgare var. leucocarpum", "Ligustrum vulgare var. auriflorum", "Ligustrum vulgare subsp. italicum", "Ligustrum vulgare f. pyramidale", "Ligustrum vulgare var. aureum", "Ligustrum vulgare f. lodense", "Ligustrum italicum", "Ligustrum vulgare f. xanthocarpum"]

description: Menodora scabra is broom-like shrub in the Olive Family (Oleaceae), known by the common name rough menodora or broom twinberry. It is a popular desert garden plant.
Notelaea cunninghamii, commonly known as black maire, is a native tree of New Zealand.
Notelaea neolanceolata, commonly known as white maire, is a tree native to New Zealand.

Notelaea ligustrina, known as the privet mock olive, native olive, doral or silkwood, is a plant in the olive family, found in southeastern Australia. It is known to grow in and near rainforests south of Monga National Park in New South Wales, and into Victoria and the island state of Tasmania. The specific epithet ligustrina refers to the Privet, which it resembles.

Notelaea longifolia is a very common shrub or small tree in eastern Australia. Occurring in or adjacent to rainforest from Mimosa Rocks National Park to Bamaga in far north Queensland. Common names include large mock-olive or long-leaved-olive. An attractive ornamental plant.

Notelaea microcarpa is a bush or small crooked tree from the olive family, found in eastern Australia. Two varieties are recognised; var. microcarpa, the velvet mock olive and var. velutina known as the gorge mock olive.

Notelaea venosa is a very common shrub or small tree in eastern Australia. Occurring in or adjacent to rainforest from Lakes Entrance, Victoria to Cunninghams Gap in south eastern Queensland. Common names include veined mock-olive, smooth mock-olive, large-leaved mock-olive and large mock-olive. Often seen in the bushland areas in Sydney.

Olea capensis, the black ironwood, is an African tree species in the olive family Oleaceae. It is widespread in sub-Saharan Africa: from the east in Somalia, Ethiopia and Sudan, south to the tip of South Africa, and west to Cameroon, Sierra Leone and the islands of the Gulf of Guinea, as well as Madagascar and the Comoros. It occurs in bush, littoral scrub and evergreen forest.
origin: Mediterranean, Africa, and South Central China

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: The olive has no serious insect pests or diseases.
Monitor the tree for olive knot, verticillium wilt, and root rot.
common_pests: The olive has no serious insect pests or diseases.
Scale insects may be a problem.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) The common olive was one of the first trees to be cultivated and dates back to 2500 B.C. The olive branch is a universal symbol of peace. It is used for medicine, fuel, and food.

description: The olive is a species of subtropical evergreen tree in the family Oleaceae. Originating in Asia Minor, it is abundant throughout the Mediterranean Basin, with wild subspecies in Africa and western Asia; modern cultivars are traced primarily to the Near East, Aegean Sea, and Strait of Gibraltar. The olive is the type species for its genus, Olea, and lends its name to the Oleaceae plant family, which includes lilac, jasmine, forsythia, and ash.
origin: Mediterranean, Africa, and South Central China

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: The olive has no serious insect pests or diseases.
Monitor the tree for olive knot, verticillium wilt, and root rot.

description: Olea lancea grows as a shrub or small tree up to 6 metres (20 ft) tall.
origin: Mediterranean, Africa, and South Central China

description: Olea paniculata, commonly known as the native olive, is a plant of the genus Olea and a relative of the olive. It grows natively in Pakistan and southwestern China (Yunnan) through tropical Asia to Australia and the Pacific islands of New Caledonia, Vanuatu and Lord Howe Island.
origin: Mediterranean, Africa, and South Central China

description: Very fragrant smell like apricots, small white flowers in early spring and some in the fall appear in leaf axils. The flowers can be dried and added to tea.
origin: East Asia

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: It is frost-sensitive, and injury will affect flowering the following fall.
common_pests: Watch for aphids and scale.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) The flowers of this plant are used in teas and other beverages that are consumed in the Far East.  Extracts from the flower are highly valuable, and are used in some of the most expensive perfumes.

description: Osmanthus heterophyllus, variously known as holly osmanthus, holly olive, and false holly, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family Oleaceae, native to eastern Asia in central and southern Japan and Taiwan.
origin: South Korea, Japan, and Taiwan

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: False Holly has no serious pest or disease problems, Observe for scale and aphids.
common_pests: False Holly has no serious pest or disease problems, Observe for scale and aphids.

description: Phillyrea angustifolia, the narrow-leaved mock privet, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family Oleaceae, native to the western and central Mediterranean.

Phillyrea latifolia, commonly known as green olive tree or mock privet, is a species of tree in the family Oleaceae. It is native to the Mediterranean Basin, from Morocco and Portugal in the west, to the Levant in the east.

Picconia azorica, locally known as pau-branco, is a species of Picconia common to the majority of islands of the Portuguese archipelago of the Azores, where it is endemic. It can grow to a medium-sized tree, though it is usually found in the form of a shrub. It is threatened by habitat loss.
Fragrant purple-pink  flowers arranged in dense, terminal clusters

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: 4-to-6-inch panicles (often in pairs) of lilac-purple flowers.
origin: Northeast China / Manchuria to Korea

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: The flower blooms are followed by clusters of warty, dry, oblong, or ovoid capsules. They measure about 0.75 inches long and are initially green and then turn yellow. The capsules can persist through the winter.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Brown capsules located at the terminal tips of branches
origin: Northern China and Mongolia, far eastern Russia

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: It is somewhat resistant to mildew, scales, and borers but is susceptible to blights, leaf spots, wilt, and ringspot virus.
common_pests: It is somewhat resistant to mildew, scales, and borers but is susceptible to blights, leaf spots, wilt, and ringspot virus.
Insect pests include scales, borers, leaf miners and caterpillars.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Leaves and fruit are used to make a tonic for the treatment of sore throats.

description: Syringa vulgaris, the lilac or common lilac, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family, Oleaceae. Native to the Balkan Peninsula, it is widely cultivated for its scented flowers in Europe and North America.
origin: Balkan Peninsula

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: Powdery mildew can seriously affect the appearance of the foliage in the summer but does little permanent damage to the shrub.
Prune the center of the plant to help with the airflow.
Additional disease problems include blights, leaf spots, wilts, ringspot virus, and honey fungus.
common_pests: Insect pests include scales, borers, leaf miners, thrips, and caterpillars.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) An essential oil obtained from the flowers can be used to make perfume. Green and brown dyes can be obtained from the leaves and a green dye from the flowers.
care.edible_parts: Flowers can be eaten raw or folded into a batter and fried to make fritters.
